did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the k-means algorithm (a machine learning/clustering method) for fine segmentation of the epidermis in skin histopathological images to aid in computer-aided diagnosis of skin cancers like melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled 'RESEARCH Open Access' in the header. It presents a novel technique for epidermis segmentation using global thresholding, shape analysis, and k-means algorithm, and reports original experimental results on 64 skin histopathological images comparing performance metrics (APRE, ASEN, ASPE, DHD, DMAD) against existing techniques.

title: Epidermis segmentation in skin histopathological images based on thickness measurement and k-means algorithm
journal: EURASIP Journal on Image and Video Processing
brief_summary: This study addresses the task of epidermis segmentation in skin histopathological images to support computer-aided diagnosis of skin cancers like melanoma. The authors propose a technique that first performs coarse segmentation using global thresholding and shape analysis, followed by thickness measurement to assess quality. If the thickness exceeds a threshold, a fine segmentation pass is conducted using the k-means algorithm to enhance accuracy. The method was evaluated on a dataset of 64 whole slide images, comprising normal skins, melanocytic nevi, and skin melanomas, with ground truth provided by manual inspection. Quantitative results on 46 testing images demonstrated that the proposed technique achieved superior performance metrics, including higher precision (96.53%) and lower Hausdorff distance (86.83 pixels), compared to existing methods like GTSA, CET, and MCGT. The study concludes that the proposed approach provides more accurate epidermis segmentation and thickness estimation suitable for melanoma grading.

lesion_types_studied.details: Part of the 64-image dataset used for evaluation; 13 images were normal skins.
Part of the 64-image dataset used for evaluation; 20 images were melanocytic nevi.
Part of the 64-image dataset used for evaluation; 31 images were skin melanomas. The paper focuses on epidermis segmentation to aid in melanoma diagnosis and grading.

all_task_types.target: Epidermis area in skin histopathological images
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on automatic segmentation of the epidermis area in whole slide skin histopathological images. The method involves coarse segmentation using global thresholding and shape analysis, followed by fine segmentation using the k-means algorithm to classify pixels into epidermis and dermis classes. Performance is evaluated using segmentation metrics such as precision (APRE), sensitivity (ASEN), specificity (ASPE), Hausdorff distance (DHD), and mean absolute distance (DMAD).

datasets_used.name: Xu and Mandal Skin Histopathological Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of 64 whole slide images (WSIs) of skin biopsies from formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue blocks, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E). The images were captured using a Carl Zeiss MIRAX MIDI Scanning system at ×40 magnification and down-sampled by a factor of 32. Ground truth segmentations were obtained via manual inspection. The images were provided by Dr. Naresh Jha and Dr. Muhammad Mahmood of the University of Alberta Hospital.

use.training: Used for parameter selection (Tarea, Tratio, τ3) and coarse segmentation evaluation. Specifically, 4 normal skins, 6 melanocytic nevi, and 8 skin melanomas were randomly selected for this purpose.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation of the proposed technique and comparison with existing techniques (GTSA, CET, MCGT). The testing set consisted of the remaining 46 images.
use.other: Ground truth was provided by manual inspection for evaluation purposes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed technique and existing methods (GTSA, CET, MCGT) were based on quantitative metrics (APRE, ASEN, ASPE, DHD, DMAD) and visual inspection, without statistical significance testing.

training_set.description: 18 WSIs (4 normal skins, 6 melanocytic nevi, 8 skin melanomas) used for parameter selection and coarse segmentation evaluation.

subset_A.description: 11 WSIs with correct segmentations after coarse segmentation.

subset_B.description: 7 WSIs with incorrect segmentations (false positives in dermis) after coarse segmentation.

testing_set.description: 46 WSIs used as an independent validation set.
testing_set.notes: The proposed technique achieved higher precision (96.53%) and lower Hausdorff distance (86.83 pixels) compared to existing methods. The thickness of the epidermis mask obtained by the proposed technique was very close to the ground truth, whereas MCGT, CET, and GTSA tended to provide much larger thicknesses due to false positives in the dermis area.

architecture_used.input_size: 3200 × 3000 pixels (for a whole slide skin histopathological image)

pipeline: Coarse segmentation using global thresholding and shape analysis
Thickness measurement via line segments perpendicular to the main axis of the segmented mask
Quality assessment: If thickness > threshold (τ3), fine segmentation is triggered
Fine segmentation using k-means algorithm over coarsely segmented results

parameters.Tarea: 0.006 M (where M is the number of foreground pixels)

architecture_details.hardware: 1.80 GHz Intel Core II Duo CPU, 16 GB RAM

training_details.training_method: Parameter selection on 18 training images (4 normal skins, 6 melanocytic nevi, 8 skin melanomas)
training_details.hyperparameter_selection: Thresholds Tarea, Tratio, and τ3 were determined empirically on the training set to optimize coarse segmentation quality
training_details.training_images: 18 WSIs randomly selected from the dataset of 64 images
training_details.testing_images: 46 WSIs used as an independent validation set

classification_or_output_head.method: k-means clustering classifies pixels into epidermis and dermis classes during the fine segmentation pass

preprocessing_techniques: Down-sampling of whole slide images by a factor of 32
Selection of the red channel from H&E stained RGB images for segmentation
Removal of white background pixels using an empirical threshold (τ1 = 240)
Application of Otsu’s global thresholding technique
Elimination of false regions based on 8-connected component analysis
Morphological closing operation with a disk-shaped structuring element (radius 10 pixels)
Morphological reconstruction to fill holes in the mask
Area-based filtering to remove small regions (false positives) below threshold Tarea
Final morphological closing operation with a disk-shaped structuring element (radius 5 pixels)
conclusions: The proposed technique, which combines global thresholding, shape analysis, thickness measurement, and the k-means algorithm, provides superior epidermis segmentation performance compared to existing techniques (GTSA, CET, and MCGT).
The proposed technique achieved higher precision (96.53% on the testing set) and lower Hausdorff distance (86.83 pixels) and mean absolute distance (6.99 pixels) than the compared methods.

The epidermis thickness estimated by the proposed technique is very close to the manually labeled ground truth, whereas existing techniques (MCGT, CET, GTSA) tend to overestimate thickness due to false positives in the dermis area.

The poor performance of GTSA and CET techniques is attributed to their inability to correctly distinguish dark cell nuclei in the dermis from the epidermis using global thresholding and shape/area analysis alone.

The MCGT technique performs poorly because its closing operation fails to remove unrelated dark components (such as hair follicles, sweat glands, and nuclei clumps) in the dermis, which vary greatly in size.
The k-means algorithm in the fine segmentation module incorrectly classifies a small number of epidermis pixels as dermis pixels, resulting in a marginal drop in sensitivity compared to GTSA.
The proposed technique has higher computational complexity (4.2 seconds per image) compared to MCGT (1.5 s), CET (3.3 s), and GTSA (0.9 s) due to the additional k-means clustering step.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, private dataset of only 64 whole slide images (13 normal, 20 nevi, 31 melanoma), limiting statistical power and generalizability.
No external validation was performed; the dataset was provided by a single institution (University of Alberta Hospital), raising concerns about population or scanner bias.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance comparisons against existing techniques (GTSA, CET, MCGT).
The k-means algorithm used for fine segmentation incorrectly classifies a small number of epidermis pixels as dermis pixels, resulting in a marginal drop in sensitivity compared to the GTSA baseline.
The proposed technique has higher computational complexity (4.2 seconds per image) compared to existing methods (MCGT: 1.5s, CET: 3.3s, GTSA: 0.9s).
The method's performance is sensitive to the presence of dark components in the dermis (e.g., hair follicles, sweat glands, nuclei clumps), which global thresholding may misclassify as epidermis.
The CET technique's poor performance is attributed to its use of a linear combination of grayscale and b* channels, which was less effective than the red channel used in this study's database.
The MCGT technique performs poorly because its closing operation fails to remove unrelated dark components in the dermis that vary greatly in size.
Ground truth was obtained via manual inspection, which may introduce inter-observer variability, although the paper cites high variability in pathologist diagnosis as a motivation for the work.
